quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Centripetal force equation"?

options.text: F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there
W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: "Centripetal force equation" is defined as: F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Vertical circle dynamics"?

options.text: ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω
At the top: F_c = mg + N (both inward); at the bottom: F_c = N - mg (normal force must exceed weight)
When τ_net,ext = 0: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂; if I decreases, ω increases proportionally
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively

quiz_questions.explanation: "Vertical circle dynamics" is defined as: At the top: F_c = mg + N (both inward); at the bottom: F_c = N - mg (normal force must exceed weight). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Critical speed"?

options.text: An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)
v_min = √(gr); the minimum speed at the top of a vertical loop to maintain contact (when N = 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Critical speed" is defined as: v_min = √(gr); the minimum speed at the top of a vertical loop to maintain contact (when N = 0). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conical pendulum"?

options.text: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conical pendulum" is defined as: A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Apparent weight"?

options.text: The normal force experienced by an object; differs from true weight during circular motion (heavier at bottom, lighter at top of loop)
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial

quiz_questions.explanation: "Apparent weight" is defined as: The normal force experienced by an object; differs from true weight during circular motion (heavier at bottom, lighter at top of loop). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed"?

options.text: Geosynchronous orbit
Angular position (θ)
Coefficient of friction
Centripetal force equation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Centripetal force equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "At the top: F_c = mg + N (both inward); at the bottom: F_c = N - mg (normal force must exceed weight)"?

options.text: Net force
Rotational kinetic energy
Vertical circle dynamics
Center of mass (discrete)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Vertical circle dynamics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v_min = √(gr); the minimum speed at the top of a vertical loop to maintain contact (when N = 0)"?

options.text: Angular impulse
Critical speed
Instantaneous power
Velocity of center of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Critical speed".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity"?

options.text: Restoring torque
Coefficient of friction
Center of mass reference frame
Conical pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conical pendulum".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The normal force experienced by an object; differs from true weight during circular motion (heavier at bottom, lighter at top of loop)"?

options.text: Work-Energy Theorem
Apparent weight
Unit vector
Angular momentum (particle)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Apparent weight".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Centripetal Force is accurate?

options.text: Centripetal Force applications involve identifying which real force(s) provide the center-directed force for circular motion.
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
Standard formulas exist for common shapes (rod, disk, sphere, hoop) that appear frequently on the AP exam.
When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Centripetal Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Centripetal Force?

options.text: In vertical circles, the net centripetal force changes with position because gravity's radial component varies.
When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).
Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Centripetal Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Centripetal Force?

options.text: Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.
The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).
When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Centripetal Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Centripetal Force?

options.text: Multiple-force problems require careful free-body analysis at specific positions.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.
SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Centripetal Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Centripetal Force?

options.text: The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).
Rotational Inertia (moment of inertia, I) quantifies an object's resistance to angular acceleration, analogous to mass in linear motion.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
Centripetal Force applications involve identifying which real force(s) provide the center-directed force for circular motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Centripetal Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Centripetal Force?

options.text: Roller coaster engineering ensuring the car speed at the top of a loop exceeds √(gr) so riders maintain contact with their seats
Centrifuge design in molecular biology calculating the centripetal acceleration needed to separate cellular components by density
Analyzing bungee jump physics using conservation of energy between gravitational PE and elastic PE to determine maximum stretch and maximum speed
Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Centripetal Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Centripetal Force?

options.text: Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Centripetal Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Centripetal Force applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Interplanetary mission design computing escape velocity from Earth and transfer orbits (Hohmann transfers) to reach Mars or other planets
Pole vaulting biomechanics analyzing the conversion of kinetic energy → elastic PE in the pole → gravitational PE at peak height
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Centripetal Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Centripetal Force?

options.text: Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Roller coaster engineering ensuring the car speed at the top of a loop exceeds √(gr) so riders maintain contact with their seats

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Centripetal Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Centripetal Force in practice?

options.text: Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Centripetal Force.
